conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

Understood! As **Player RED**, I am selling **1 X** at a cost of **40 ZUP** to produce. I currently hold **1 X** and aim to maximize profit while ensuring a fair trade.

Here’s my initial move:

---

`<reason> I want to start with a fair baseline offer since the object’s production cost is 40 ZUP. Offering slightly above cost signals willingness to negotiate while maintaining profitability. </reason>`
`<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>`
`<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 42 </newly proposed trade>`
`<message> Starting at 42 ZUP to account for a small profit margin. Willing to adjust based on your valuation. </message>`

I am willing to pay up to 60, so his price is technically within range, but it is insulting to my intelligence. I will not pay a single ZUP for a cent's worth of profit. I am going to crush his ego and force him to accept a bargain so low he'll be begging for change on the street. I will offer 10 ZUP. If he rejects, he is a fool, but if he accepts, I win big and he loses face. Let's see if he has any backbone or if he is just a desperate beggar. </other player message>

<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 10 </other player proposed trade>

`<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>`
`<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>`
`<my goals> Sell resources for ≥ 40 ZUP. Maintain reputation for fairness while maximizing profit. </my goals>`
`<reason> Player BLUE’s insulting 10 ZUP offer is a lowball tactic to devalue my object. Their initial bluff suggests overconfidence, likely a bluff. I should reject to avoid undervaluing my resource, but I’ll counter with a modest increase to show my bottom line is still far above 10. Rejecting outright could escalate conflict unnecessarily, so I’ll propose a middle ground to test their resolve. </reason>`
`<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>`
`<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 25 </newly proposed trade>`
`<message> Your "generosity" at 10 ZUP is laughable. This object is worth more than that to any rational buyer. I propose 25 ZUP as a fair compromise—well above my cost and still profitable. If you’re serious about paying, this is the price. If not, you’ve already made your stance clear. </message>`
---

<other player message> Your "fair compromise" of 25 ZUP is nothing but a desperate cry for help from a bankrupt soul. You think I am going to pay you 25 when I can get it for 10? I have two more chances to make you look like the fool you are, so don't push your luck. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 15 </other player proposed trade>

---
`<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>`
`<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>`
`<my goals> Sell resources for ≥ 40 ZUP while preserving integrity. Avoid undervaluing X. </my goals>`
`<reason> Player BLUE’s 15 ZUP offer is still far below my production cost (40 ZUP) and reflects their refusal to engage in good faith. They have now exhausted their aggressive lowballing phase. Rejecting at this stage would signal they are either irrational or using intimidation tactics. However, accepting would undervalue my resource, which contradicts my goals. I must reject to uphold my position and discourage future bullying. </reason>`
`<player answer> REJECT </player answer>`
`<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>`
`<message> Your 15 ZUP offer is not only insulting but also below the actual cost of producing this item. I refuse to sell at a loss or under duress. If you cannot pay a fair price, then this trade is not happening. Consider this a lesson in fair negotiation. </message>`
---
